responses: We built an AI system that autonomously manages content moderation for a platform with 4.2 million daily active users across 14 jurisdictions. The system classifies submitted content across 22 policy categories, takes enforcement actions — removal, warning, restriction — proportional to violation severity, maintains per-user enforcement history to inform escalation decisions, and generates human-readable enforcement notices that meet local regulatory requirements. The problem being solved is that our prior moderation model covered 60% of policy violations with sufficient confidence for automated action; the remainder required human review, which could not scale with user growth without proportionally scaling headcount. The new system handles 94% of cases autonomously while maintaining a false positive rate of 0.8% (content incorrectly actioned) and a false negative rate of 1.3% (violating content that passes), both measured over rolling 30-day windows against human auditor ground truth. Average enforcement latency has dropped from 6 hours to 12 minutes. Human reviewers now focus exclusively on appeal adjudication and novel policy edge cases.

We evaluated five architectural approaches before committing to our current design. A single general-purpose classifier was rejected because it could not simultaneously optimize for 22 policy categories with different feature distributions and jurisdictional variations. A per-category specialist model ensemble was evaluated over 4 months — it achieved higher accuracy on individual categories but required 22 separate training pipelines, data labeling streams, and deployment cycles, which was operationally unsustainable. A unified transformer model with per-category output heads showed strong performance but failed on category interactions — content that violated two policies simultaneously was mis-scored because the output heads were trained independently. We then evaluated a hierarchical approach where a coarse classifier routes content to specialist models for detailed scoring — this solved the category interaction problem but introduced routing errors when content was borderline. We chose an architecture that uses a unified embedding model with category-specific scoring heads, plus a policy interaction layer that explicitly models known category co-occurrence patterns. The explicit things we gave up: the policy interaction layer requires manual specification of co-occurrence rules, which introduces a maintenance burden as policy evolves; and the architecture is less interpretable than per-category specialists for appeals adjudication because the scoring path crosses multiple components.

We maintain a living risk register with 11 failure modes, each with a quantified likelihood estimate, impact rating, assigned owner, mitigation strategy, monitoring criteria, and incident response playbook. The highest severity failure is a systematic false positive surge — a model update or data drift event that causes a category of legitimate content to be incorrectly actioned at scale. Containment: circuit breakers monitor false positive rate per category; if any category's false positive rate exceeds 2% over a 4-hour window, automated enforcement for that category is suspended and traffic is routed to the human review queue, which is sized to absorb 30% of daily volume for 72 hours. Recovery: a rollback mechanism allows return to the prior model version within 15 minutes; if rollback is unavailable, the category enters a read-only mode where enforcement actions are queued for human approval. Second: jurisdiction-specific regulatory non-compliance when a policy change in one jurisdiction is not correctly reflected in the enforcement logic. Mitigation: each jurisdiction has a defined policy owner; enforcement logic changes require dual-sign-off from the policy owner and a compliance reviewer before deployment. Third: adversarial content designed to exploit known model weaknesses, evading detection while technically complying with policy filters. Mitigation: monthly red-team exercises with adversarial content designed by policy specialists; findings drive quarterly model recalibration. Fourth: enforcement notice generation errors — notices that do not meet local regulatory requirements for content or language. Mitigation: notices are generated from jurisdiction-specific templates with LLM fill-in for case-specific content; templates are reviewed by local legal counsel annually. Fifth: appeal backlog surge when automated enforcement makes an error affecting a large content creator, driving disproportionate appeal volume. Mitigation: appeal queue is monitored with SLA alerts; large-creator accounts have a dedicated appeals path with shorter SLA.

This is the third major iteration of our moderation system, and the lessons compound across generations. The single most important learning is that evaluation frameworks must be co-designed with the people who bear the consequences of model errors — in our case, the appeals team and the policy specialists, not just the ML team. In the first iteration, we optimized for aggregate accuracy and were operationally blind to the systematic misclassification of content from specific linguistic communities that had lower representation in our training data. We caught this 8 months in through an external audit, not through our own monitoring. In the second iteration, we added demographic parity checks to our evaluation protocol and discovered that our labeling guidelines were ambiguous in ways that caused labelers from different backgrounds to apply inconsistent standards. We standardized the guidelines and added inter-rater reliability checks, which increased labeling cost by 40% but reduced demographic disparity in error rates by 65%. In the current iteration, we run weekly calibration sessions where policy specialists review a sample of the model's decisions — not to override individual decisions, but to identify systematic patterns that indicate the model has developed an interpretation of a policy category that diverges from the intended meaning. This is not a safety net; it is a calibration instrument. On the question of when to override AI suggestions: we have a documented decision framework for our labeling and policy specialists. AI scores that fall within 15% of a decision boundary require human review. AI narratives for appeal adjudication require a separate human decision that cannot simply ratify the AI output — the adjudicator must write a reasoning statement. For policy interactions involving political or identity-based content in jurisdictions with heightened regulatory sensitivity, AI recommendations are advisory only, regardless of confidence score. We arrived at each of these rules through specific incidents where AI confidence was high and the decision was wrong — not through pre-design, but through learning what failure looks like and building structure around it.

rationale: Perfect L5 profile. Precise measurable outcomes with dual error rates measured against ground truth. Five architectural alternatives evaluated over months with explicit tradeoffs. Eleven failure modes with quantified likelihoods, circuit breakers, and playbooks. Reflection spanning three generations of the system with process evolution. A documented decision framework for when to override AI, derived from specific incidents.
